output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.95, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.4, "R1.7": 0.3}}

Summary: The section has several internal contradictions serious enough to undermine the contribution claims; I flag them and recommend specific fixes.

**1. The "unsupervised" claim is contradicted by the experimental protocol.**
The Method paragraph states the pipeline is "fully unsupervised" and that "no labeled instances of biased or unbiased judging are required at any stage." The Experiments paragraph then says the threshold τ is chosen by sweeping to maximize F1 on a 1,200-pair calibration set with human annotations of self-preference. Threshold calibration on labeled bias data is supervision — exactly the kind the Method paragraph rules out. Two ways to fix:
   - Reframe honestly as "label-efficient" or "weakly supervised at the threshold-selection step," and quantify how much labeled data is needed.
   - Or, derive τ from the theoretical false-alarm rate (you claim formal guarantees — use them), and treat the human-annotated set purely as evaluation, not calibration. Then re-report F1.

**2. The "formal detection guarantees" claim is asserted but not substantiated, and is in tension with the empirical thresholding.**
The opening promises a proof that biased judges are flagged with high probability and unbiased judges incur a controlled false-alarm rate "under mild assumptions." If those guarantees exist, the threshold should be set analytically (e.g., from the χ² tail of Mahalanobis distance under the Gaussian null), not by F1-sweeping on labels. Either:
   - Show the theorem statement (or point to where it lives) and use it to set τ, then report whether the empirical FAR matches the predicted bound.
   - Or, soften the claim — "we provide a probabilistic motivation" rather than "formal detection guarantees."

**3. The contribution framing shifts mid-section.**
The opening says the core contribution is a detector with formal guarantees. The closing says the "principal deliverable" is the 8,400-pair benchmark. These are different contributions, and a reader cannot tell which the paper is actually about. Pick one as primary and demote the other, or explicitly say "two contributions: (i) … (ii) …" up front.

**4. Evidence-claim mismatches on the headline result.**
   - "0.81 F1" with τ tuned on a labeled set and tested on a disjoint split is a reasonable empirical result, but you currently have no baseline. The claim "first unsupervised anomaly-detection framework" makes baseline-free reporting tempting, but a simple comparator (e.g., a thresholded raw self-vs-panel score gap, or a per-judge t-test on residuals) would let the reader see what the Mahalanobis machinery buys.
   - "Strongest gains on judges from the GPT and Claude families" — gains relative to what? With no baseline this phrase is unsupported. Either add a baseline or rephrase as "highest detection scores were on…".
   - The "to our knowledge no prior work has cast self-preference bias as a distributional anomaly-detection problem" claim needs at least a footnote distinguishing this from existing self-preference work (Panickssery et al., Zheng et al. MT-Bench, etc.) and from generic LLM-judge bias detection.

**5. Setup is missing a key definition.**
You define r_{ij} as "deviation of judge i's score on its own output from the panel mean on the same pair." This presupposes that on item j we know which output is judge i's own — i.e., the pairs are structured so each pair contains a candidate from judge i (as a generator) being scored by judge i (as a judge). Say this explicitly. Otherwise readers will read S_{ij} as a generic pairwise-preference matrix and the residual definition becomes incoherent.

**6. Smaller issues.**
   - "Multivariate Gaussian to the residual vectors" — vectors over what dimension? Over the M items? Over the N judges? You need N ≥ dimension+something for the covariance to be well-conditioned; mention regularization or shrinkage if used.
   - "8,400 response pairs sampled from MT-Bench-style multi-turn dialogues" — "MT-Bench-style" is vague. Either it is MT-Bench (cite Zheng et al. 2023) or it is a new corpus (describe its construction).
   - The held-out / test split sizes don't add up cleanly: 1,200 calibration + "disjoint test split" of unspecified size out of 8,400 — state the test split size.
   - "Nine open- and closed-weight judges" — list them, or at least the families and sizes.

**Recommended structural change.** Move the contribution restatement to a single sentence at the end of the opening paragraph, listing (i) the framing as anomaly detection, (ii) the detector, (iii) the benchmark, in priority order. Then the Experiments paragraph should not introduce a new "principal deliverable."

Uncertainty: I have not verified whether prior work (e.g., Panickssery et al. on LLM self-preference, or broader LLM-judge bias literature) has cast this as anomaly detection — the novelty claim should be checked against that literature before publication. I also assumed standard Mahalanobis/χ² thresholding semantics; if the paper's "formal guarantees" use a different probabilistic device, point 2 should be re-evaluated against the actual theorem.
